description: T621 SagaOrchestrator MUST use storeDocumentWithOCC(versionPin:-1) at ORDER 1 to prevent crash-replay saga re-initialization. Plain storeDocument allows replay to reset saga to INIT state, losing all completed steps and checkpoint data.
blockConditions: T621 uses plain storeDocument for saga init instead of storeDocumentWithOCC
T621 does not include versionPin:-1 to detect existing RUNNING saga
OCC_CONFLICT on crashed saga ignored instead of returning idempotent SagaAlreadyRunning
passConditions: T621 calls storeDocumentWithOCC(versionPin:-1) at executionOrder=1 for saga init
OCC_CONFLICT returns DataProcessResult.success({status:'ALREADY_RUNNING'}) — idempotent
Crashed saga recovers without resetting stepIndex to 0

description: T621 SagaOrchestrator MUST acquire SETNX(step-lock:{sagaId}:{stepIndex}) at ORDER 2 BEFORE executing step body. On crash recovery, parallel instances competing for the same step MUST use the lock to ensure only one executes.
blockConditions: T621 acquires step lock AFTER step body executes
T621 does not use SETNX for step-level deduplication
Step body may execute multiple times if lock check happens after completion
passConditions: T621 acquires SETNX step-lock at executionOrder=2 before step body
Lock collision returns immediate skip without re-executing step business logic
Parallel crash recovery instances serialize via step lock

description: T621 SagaOrchestrator MUST track completedSteps array in saga state and persist after each step succeeds. Operator recovery requires inspecting which steps completed and which failed to understand saga's current business state.
blockConditions: T621 does not persist completedSteps after each step
Saga state omits checkpoint data for operator recovery
Crash recovery has no way to determine which steps have completed
passConditions: T621 updates completedSteps array at ORDER 3 after each step
Saga document persists with up-to-date completedSteps checkpoint
Operator can inspect sagaId and view exact execution progress

description: T621 SagaOrchestrator MUST resolve tenantId from AsyncLocalStorage ONLY. Accepting tenantId from saga payload enables cross-tenant saga hijacking, allowing one tenant to manipulate another tenant's compensations.

T621 reads tenantId from saga event payload instead of ALS

passConditions: T621 resolves tenantId exclusively from AsyncLocalStorage

description: T622 CompensationEngine MUST execute compensations in LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) order via [...compensationStack].reverse(). Later steps depend on earlier state — undoing step N before N-1 leaves partial state. Parallel compensation (Promise.all) is forbidden because step dependencies are unknown.
blockConditions: T622 executes compensation in FIFO order (original stack order)
T622 uses Promise.all for parallel compensation execution
Compensation order logic omitted — default array iteration
passConditions: T622 defines COMPENSATION_STEPS_LIFO = [...stack].reverse() as const
Compensation loop uses serial for..of (no Promise.all)
Each compensation executes in reverse order of originating steps

description: T622 CompensationEngine MUST implement stop-on-first-failure semantics: if compensation step N fails, emit CompensationFailed and HALT immediately. Continuing after failure leaves saga in partially-compensated undefined state with unclear recovery path.
blockConditions: T622 implements best-effort continuation on compensation failure
T622 continues loop after compensation step fails
Multiple failures silently accumulated without halting
passConditions: T622 checks compResult.isSuccess() after each compensation step
Failure triggers CompensationFailed event emission and immediate return
Loop halts on first failure — no best-effort continued processing

description: T622 CompensationEngine MUST track completedCompensations array and emit CompensationProgress event after each successful step. Operator monitoring requires visibility into which compensations have completed and which remain pending.

Operator has no visibility into compensation execution progress
No checkpoint data available for manual recovery decision-making
passConditions: T622 updates completedCompensations array after each successful step

Operator can inspect sagaId and determine compensation progress in real time

description: T623 ComplianceAuditWriter MUST compute retentionExpiresAt at write time from FREEDOM config key 'flow19_compliance_retention_days'. Deferred computation (batch job) means record has no retention policy during early life. If policy changes between write and deferred compute, wrong expiry stored, breaking legal compliance.
blockConditions: T623 stores compliance record without retentionExpiresAt field
T623 uses deferred batch job to compute retention expiry later
Retention days hardcoded instead of from FREEDOM config

description: T623 ComplianceAuditWriter MUST append compliance records in append-only index (xiigen-compliance-audit). No deleteDocument or updateDocument. Append-only immutability is required for legal defensibility — once recorded, compliance events cannot be altered.
blockConditions: T623 calls deleteDocument on xiigen-compliance-audit index

description: T623 ComplianceAuditWriter is PLATFORM_ONLY service — never tenant-scoped. Compliance audits must aggregate cross-tenant saga failures and compensation events. Tenant-scoped compliance records would hide failures in other tenants.

passConditions: T623 is PLATFORM_ONLY service — no tenantId parameter accepted

description: T624 DataRetentionManager MUST implement dual-gate retention: (1) archive gate at retentionExpiresAt BEFORE delete, (2) permanent delete gate after archive_retention_days expire. Immediate deletion loses forensic data for disputes and regulatory audits. Archive first ensures data is retained for dispute resolution window.
blockConditions: T624 deletes records immediately when retentionExpiresAt passes

No archive_retention_days delay between archive and deletion
passConditions: T624 archives records to cold storage at retentionExpiresAt

description: T624 DataRetentionManager MUST use SETNX(purge-lock:{tenantId}:{retentionPolicy}) at ORDER 1 to prevent parallel purge jobs from competing for the same deletion work. If two purge instances run simultaneously for the same tenant, they may delete overlapping record sets, causing missed deletes or redundant processing.
blockConditions: T624 purge logic has no SETNX deduplication lock
